answer:
**Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (unknown author, ancient Sanskrit verse) is the primary text in the corpus that systematically classifies sins by severity, particularly in the context of determining the size of the assembly (*pariṣad*) required for judgment and expiation, as well as penance durations. It distinguishes between:

- **Greatest sins** (requiring a thousand-member assembly).[^1]
- **Great sins** (requiring a hundred-member assembly or more).[^2]
- **Secondary sins** (e.g., killing a cow; requiring a fifty-member assembly; said to be innumerable).[^3]
- **Minor sins** (requiring a small assembly).[^4]

It explicitly enumerates some major sins, equating killers of women/men, defilers of maidens, and cow-killers with the archetypal Brahmin-killer (*brāhmaṇa-hantā*) as "fallen" (*patitāḥ*). A section is titled "The Enumeration of Sins" (Chapter 7 in its internal structure).[^5]

**No other works** in the corpus provide a comparable classification of sins. Mentions of "sin" or "sins" appear elsewhere (e.g., poetically in *Andreas* by Cynewulf; legalistically in *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*), but without systematic categorization or enumeration.[^6]

[^1]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, ch. 1, [p-5068cf](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-5068cf).
[^2]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, ch. 1, [p-47bbf4](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-47bbf4).
[^3]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, ch. 1, [p-5068cf](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-5068cf) and [p-39c7b4](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-39c7b4).
[^4]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, ch. 1, [p-5068cf](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-5068cf).
[^5]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, ch. 1, [p-9c7e63](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-9c7e63).
[^6]: E.g., *Andreas*, ch. 1 (narrative uses); *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, ch. 1, [p-182b0c](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-182b0c) ("accusations of great sins").
